content.text:
The completeness axiom has far-reaching consequences. In this section, 
we derive several fundamental properties of $\mathbb{R}$.

content.title: Archimedean Property
content.content: For any $x \in \mathbb{R}$, there exists $n \in \mathbb{N}$ such that $n > x$.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
Suppose, for contradiction, that $x$ is an upper bound for $\mathbb{N}$. 
By completeness, $\alpha = \sup \mathbb{N}$ exists.

Since $\alpha - 1 < \alpha$, the number $\alpha - 1$ is not an upper bound 
for $\mathbb{N}$ (by minimality of supremum). Thus there exists 
$n \in \mathbb{N}$ with $n > \alpha - 1$, which means $n + 1 > \alpha$.

But $n + 1 \in \mathbb{N}$, contradicting that $\alpha$ is an upper bound 
for $\mathbb{N}$.

content.text:
The Archimedean property says that $\mathbb{N}$ is unbounded—no real number 
is larger than all natural numbers. This seems obvious, but it *requires* 
the completeness axiom.

content.title: Corollaries of the Archimedean Property
content.content:
1. For any $\varepsilon > 0$, there exists $n \in \mathbb{N}$ such that 
$\displaystyle\frac{1}{n} < \varepsilon$.

2. For any $x > 0$, there exists $n \in \mathbb{N}$ such that $n - 1 \leq x < n$.

3. $\displaystyle\inf\left\{\frac{1}{n} : n \in \mathbb{N}\right\} = 0$.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
(a) By the Archimedean property, there exists $n \in \mathbb{N}$ with 
$n > 1/\varepsilon$, i.e., $1/n < \varepsilon$.

(b) By the Archimedean property, the set $\{n \in \mathbb{N} : n > x\}$ is 
nonempty. By the well-ordering of $\mathbb{N}$, it has a least element, 
say $m$. Then $m > x$ and $m - 1 \leq x$.

(c) Let $S = \{1/n : n \in \mathbb{N}\}$. Clearly $0$ is a lower bound. 
If $\ell > 0$ were a greater lower bound, then by part (a), there would 
exist $n$ with $1/n < \ell$, contradicting that $\ell$ is a lower bound.

content.title: Density of $\mathbb{Q
content.content:
$ in $R$}

Between any two distinct real numbers, there exists a rational number.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
Let $a < b$ be real numbers. We seek a rational $r = m/n$ with $a < m/n < b$.

By the Archimedean property, there exists $n \in \mathbb{N}$ with 
$n(b - a) > 1$, i.e., $nb - na > 1$. This means the interval $(na, nb)$ 
has length greater than $1$, so it contains an integer $m$.

Then $na < m < nb$, which gives $a < m/n < b$.

content.title: Density of Irrationals
content.content: Between any two distinct real numbers, there exists an irrational number.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
Let $a < b$. By Theorem [thm:Q-dense], there exists a rational $r$ with 
$\displaystyle\frac{a}{\sqrt{2}} < r < \frac{b}{\sqrt{2}}$.

Then $a < r\sqrt{2} < b$. Since $r \neq 0$ (we can choose such an $r$) and 
$\sqrt{2}$ is irrational, $r\sqrt{2}$ is irrational.

content.text:
These density theorems tell us that both the rationals and irrationals are 
"spread throughout" the real line. You cannot find an interval, no matter 
how small, that contains only rationals or only irrationals.

content.title: Existence of $n$th Roots
content.content:
For every $x > 0$ and every $n \in \mathbb{N}$, there exists a unique 
$y > 0$ such that $y^n = x$. We write $y = x^{1/n}$ or $y = \sqrt[n]{x}$.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
**Uniqueness:** If $y_1^n = y_2^n = x$ with $y_1, y_2 > 0$ and 
$y_1 \neq y_2$, say $y_1 < y_2$, then $y_1^n < y_2^n$ (since $t \mapsto t^n$ 
is strictly increasing for $t > 0$), contradiction.

**Existence:** Let $S = \{t \in \mathbb{R} : t > 0 \text{ and } t^n < x\}$.

$S$ is nonempty: if $x \geq 1$, then $1/2 \in S$; if $x < 1$, then $x \in S$ 
(since $x^n < x$ for $0 < x < 1$ and $n \geq 1$).

$S$ is bounded above: if $t > x + 1$, then $t^n \geq t > x$, so $x + 1$ is 
an upper bound.

Let $y = \sup S$. We claim $y^n = x$.

**Case 1:** Suppose $y^n < x$. We will find $\varepsilon > 0$ such that 
$(y + \varepsilon)^n < x$, contradicting that $y$ is an upper bound for $S$.

By the binomial theorem,
\[
(y + \varepsilon)^n = y^n + \binom{n}{1}y^{n-1}\varepsilon + \cdots + \varepsilon^n < y^n + \varepsilon[(1+y)^n - y^n]
\]
for small $\varepsilon$. Choose $\varepsilon$ small enough that this is less 
than $x$.

**Case 2:** Suppose $y^n > x$. A similar argument shows that 
$y - \varepsilon$ is still an upper bound for $S$ for some $\varepsilon > 0$, 
contradicting that $y = \sup S$.

Thus $y^n = x$.

content.title: Richard Dedekind (1831--1916)
content.content:
The rigorous treatment of real numbers was largely developed by Richard 
Dedekind. His 1872 paper introduced what we now call *Dedekind cuts*: 
a way to construct each real number as a partition of the rationals. This 
finally put the real number system on solid logical foundations, after 
centuries of intuitive but imprecise use.

marginNotes.text:
Who has measured the waters in the hollow of 
his hand, or with the breadth of his hand marked off the heavens?
